navigation.title: Source trace
navigation.description: What Seonic can trace today, what remains route-level, and where the model should get sharper.

metadata.title: Source Trace
metadata.description: Understand how Seonic identifies metadata sources today and what stronger source trace should mean for the product.

hero.title: Source trace starts with provenance
hero.lead: Seonic is most useful when a warning tells you not only that something is wrong, but also how that value entered the route. Today the product already tracks important provenance signals; the next step is making that trail more precise.

sections.title: Why it matters
sections.lead: An issue list without provenance creates extra search work for the engineer who has to fix it.

items.title: Less guessing
items.body: It is not enough to say that a canonical URL is missing. Teams need to know whether the route is fully unconfigured, partially detected, or already backed by existing Next.js metadata.

items.title: Faster fixes
items.body: The shorter the path from a warning to the route file or metadata source, the easier it is to move from inspection to change.

items.title: Safer adoption
items.body: Teams can adopt Seonic incrementally when they can see what came from existing metadata and what still needs explicit Seonic configuration.

sections.title: What Seonic can trace today
sections.lead: Current provenance is strongest at the route and source-model level rather than the exact field-and-line level.

items.title: Route discovery
items.body: Seonic maps route paths to files in app/ or src/app/ and keeps track of dynamic segments.

items.title: Metadata source detection
items.body: For each route, Seonic can detect whether values came from a static metadata export, a dynamic generateMetadata function, Seonic config, or a merged combination.

items.title: Live server override
items.body: When devServer.url is configured, rendered metadata fetched from the running Next.js app can replace earlier static assumptions for supported routes.

items.title: Configuration guidance
items.body: In the UI, configured, detected, and unconfigured states drive the guidance for generating or creating .seonic.ts files.

sections.title: How that shows up in the product
sections.lead: Even before field-level trace is complete, provenance already changes how the dashboard is read.

items: Routes can be separated into configured, detected, and unconfigured groups.
Detected routes are labeled as static metadata or dynamic metadata so the team knows what kind of Next.js source exists already.
Warnings and score calculations are skipped for routes that are still completely unconfigured, which keeps the dashboard honest about what is missing versus what is merely weak.
Generate flows can take detected data and turn it into route-level Seonic files instead of forcing teams to start from empty config.

sections.title: What stronger source trace should mean
sections.lead: The long-term goal is not a vague "source trace" badge. It is a materially shorter path from a bad route preview to the exact code that should change.

blocks.title: Current limit
blocks.content: Today Seonic mostly traces provenance at the route, config, and metadata-source level. Precise field-level trace back to a symbol, export, or exact code location is still a product direction rather than a finished surface.

That distinction matters because it keeps the docs honest. The current product can already explain what kind of source exists and which routes are governed by it. The next layer is showing the exact place engineers should open next.

cta.title: Return to the broader model
cta.lead: Go back to the product overview for the full inspection loop: route scan, validation, previews, and generated outputs.
